name: VSFP3.1
description: Third-generation Ci-VSP voltage sensor using a single Cerulean fluorescent protein, derived from VSFP2 by removing the FRET acceptor, achieving the fastest response kinetics reported among fluorescent protein-based voltage indicators at the time.

spectrum.name: VSFP3.1
spectrum.note: Cerulean (A206K) is VSFP3.1's only chromophore (the FRET acceptor YFP was removed from VSFP2A R217Q). spectrum.custom is the verbatim FPbase Cerulean excitation+emission curve (Pearson r = 1.0000, max|diff| = 0.0000 vs FPbase; peaks 435/477 nm match). The paper itself (Lundby 2008 Fig. 2c, Perron 2009 Fig. 5C) shows only the Cerulean emission band; no full independent ex/em spectrum was published for VSFP3.1.

voltage.name: VSFP3.1
voltage.note: Re-digitized 2026-06-13 from Lundby 2008 Fig. S3a, the normalized F-V (ΔF/ΔFmax, 0→1) for VSFP3.1 cyan fluorescence over −110→+110 mV (Boltzmann V½ = −51.2 mV, slope a = 20.8 mV, n=7). The figure is a NORMALIZED magnitude curve (not signed ΔF/F), so each digitized marker n(V) was rescaled to signed ΔF/F via the project's normalized-Boltzmann method: ΔF/F(V) = −D·[n(V) − n(−70)], with D = 1.9% (the total ΔF/F amplitude, Perron 2009 Chem Biol Table 1) and n(−70) = 0.264 read off the curve, giving the site-wide −70 mV = 0 normalization. Negative-going (F decreases on depolarization). The prior sourceImage/sourceFigure ('Fig. 5C') was WRONG — it was the ΔF/F-vs-time kinetics response trace (from Lundby Fig. 2c / Perron Fig. 5C), not an F-V curve; replaced with the real Fig. S3a F-V panel. Note the sensor is already ~26% activated at −70 mV (V½ ≈ −51 mV), so the −70→+30 mV (100-mV) step is only ~−1.3% of the 1.9% full-range swing.

kinetics.note: Activation time constant 1.3 ± 0.1 ms at +70 mV (n=7), 25°C — this is the DOMINANT FAST component of a bi-exponential fluorescence response (the paper notes 'a dominant time-constant that closely tracks the activation time-course of the charge movement, and a less prominent slower component resembling the FRET component'). The slow component is NOT quantified numerically in Lundby 2008, so a proper amplitude-weighted mean cannot be computed from this paper; the headline 1.3 ms is the fast component alone. No deactivation (τ_off) is reported in Lundby 2008. See the Chem Biol Table 1 entry for a fully resolved bi-exponential ON fit at 35°C.

Amplitude-weighted mean ON τ for VSFP3.1_Cerulean from the double-exponential fit (Perron 2009 Chem Biol Table 1, at -20 mV, 35°C, n>=10 cells): fast τ_ON 1.8 ± 0.3 ms contributing 69% + slow τ_ON 77.5 ± 34 ms contributing 31% -> 0.69x1.8 + 0.31x77.5 = 25.3 ms. The fast component (1.8 ms) alone matches Lundby's 1.3 ms fast component; the weighted mean is dominated by the large slow component. Deactivation (τ_off) for VSFP3.1_Cerulean is reported only in Table S1 (supplementary, not available in the accessible PDF), so off is omitted. ON time constants are voltage-dependent (fast τ peaks near V½_total -43.6 mV).

sensitivityData.note: Simulation-predicted single-action-potential optical response ΔF/F0 for VSFP3.1: 0.05%–0.1% per spike (stored value 0.075% is the midpoint of the reported range). Cross-measured/modeled in the VSFP2.3 paper (Akemann et al. 2009 Biophys J, doi:10.1016/j.bpj.2009.02.046), which built a four-state kinetic model of VSFP3.1 from its measured sensing/gating currents and fluorescence responses (25°C data, q10-scaled), then simulated spike-evoked fluorescence in cerebellar Purkinje and layer-5 pyramidal neuron models (Model III, 500 VSDs/µm²). Despite robust reporter activation (3–10% per spike, Fig. 9 C1), the differential fluorescence per spike is only 0.05–0.1% — too small to overcome photon shot noise in a single trial (80-trial averaging needed). 1P optical readout, computational prediction (not a direct experimental ΔF/F-per-AP measurement).

dynamicRangeData.note: Stored value is the −70→+30 mV (100-mV) step, −1.3% (rule 1d window). The PUBLISHED dynamic range is ΔF/F = 1.9% (Perron 2009 Chem Biol Table 1, 'Dynamic Range' column) — the D (amplitude) parameter of the Boltzmann fit ΔF/F = A + (D−A)/(1+exp((Vm−V½)/s)) over the FULL −140→+60 mV span at 35°C (V½_total = −43.6 mV), i.e. a >100-mV range, so it overstates the per-100-mV step. Because VSFP3.1 is already ~26% activated at −70 mV (V½ ≈ −51 mV, Lundby Fig. S3a), the −70→+30 mV step captures ~0.71 of the full 1.9% swing → ~−1.3%, which matches the rescaled F-V curve (voltage.custom). Negative-going (fluorescence decreases on depolarization), consistent with voltage.polarity. 1P widefield/photometry, PC12 cells.

brightnessData.source: Estimated from Cerulean EC=43000, QY=0.62 vs EGFP EC=56000, QY=0.60
brightnessData.note: Curator estimate, not a measured figure: brightness ratio = (Cerulean EC×QY)/(EGFP EC×QY) = (43000×0.62)/(56000×0.60) = 26660/33600 = 0.79. Cerulean and EGFP photophysical constants (EC, QY) are the FPbase/literature values; VSFP3.1's papers do not report a measured brightness comparison to EGFP. Since VSFP3.1 uses Cerulean as its sole chromophore, this is the component-FP brightness proxy.
